applied_optimizations.name: Triton fused kernel with block-level reduction
applied_optimizations.description: Implemented MSE loss using Triton kernel that fuses subtraction and squaring operations, with block-level reduction. Each block computes partial sum of squared differences, then final reduction done on small block_sums array. BLOCK_SIZE=65536 (multiple of 64 for AMD wavefront alignment). Key fixes from failed attempt: (1) avoided atomic_add which doesn't work correctly for multi-block reduction, (2) added explicit .to(tl.float32) casts as required by ROCm Triton, (3) proper two-level reduction strategy.

attempted_but_failed.name: Elementwise kernel with PyTorch reduction
attempted_but_failed.error: Writing intermediate squared_diff tensor to global memory then reducing separately was slower than PyTorch's three separate kernels due to extra memory traffic

notes: Triton kernel fuses subtraction and squaring into single kernel, eliminating memory traffic between operations
PyTorch reference uses 3 separate kernels: sub (2.20ms), pow (1.41ms), mean (1.09ms) = 4.70ms total
Our Triton kernel: 1.69ms for fused compute + negligible reduction overhead = 1.77ms total
BLOCK_SIZE=65536 works well for this problem size (1,073,741,824 elements / 65536 = 16384 blocks)

attempted_but_failed.name: trial_4
attempted_but_failed.reason: Trials 2, 3, and 4 all produced no output — the agent is crashing before completing. Need extremely minimal instructions: verify existing kernel first, then make one small change at a time.
